tips.body: Crash the plane, don't land it — angle the kubb's nose down so it digs into the ground rather than tumbling away.
tips.quote: When drilling a kubb, don't land the shuttle, you want to crash the plane.

tips.body: Spin the kubb like a tight football spiral — but underarm, and with the nose pointed downward. That's what makes it stick.
tips.quote: Think about throwing a football and putting a tight spin on the ball. That is the kubb, except it is underarm and the rotation is the opposite direction.

tips.body: Generate rotation from one finger, not the whole hand or arm. Place your middle finger under the kubb and roll it from bottom to top.
tips.quote: The rotation comes not from the arm or the hand. For me its only one digit. The longest one. I put this finger under the kubb and then I move from bottom to top.

tips.body: Find your spin happy medium. Too much spin is impossible to control. Too little and the kubb won't stay straight.
Start with a softer touch. Too much power and too much spin both make the kubb impossible to control. Throw less, control more.
Place your first kubb on the centerline near the pin so it's forced in-bounds. Use it as a marker and backstop for the rest of your throws.
tips.quote: My first kubb I will try to place it ON the centerline, close to the pin, so that it is forced in bounds. I then try to 'skip' my kubbs in right behind it.

tips.body: Drill your first kubb with no movement. Then bounce the next ones into it from the right to build a tight cluster.
tips.quote: I try to drill the first kubb(s) with no movement. Then, the following kubbs would roll to the right after they hit the ground.
tips.attributionLong: Scott Forster, team Default (2010 U.S. Championship runners-up)

tips.body: Follow through with your arm after release — raise it high like a clean pendulum. Like golf: hit through the kubb, not at it.
tips.quote: Maybe there's an analogy here with golf; you want to hit through the ball, rather than hit on it.

tips.body: Never be afraid to practice drilling from a shorter distance until you're consistent. Work your way back to 8m gradually.
To place a kubb close behind ones already on the field, dip the front end down and throw looser than normal.
tips.quote: If I would like to place the kubb close behind the kubbs already lying on the field I dip the front end and angle the kubb towards the ground and throw the kubb looser than normally.
tips.attributionLong: Joakim Ekelöf, Team Ekeby (considered by many the best kubb player in the world)

tips.body: Drill kubbs onto the lines and into the corner. The fewer alignment choices your opponent has when raising kubbs, the more your team will knock down.
Aim to cluster field kubbs close together with a high arc. Grouped kubbs are far easier to knock down with a single baton.
Good inkasting is throwing evenly all the time — and knowing how to clean up the mess when a kubb doesn't land where you planned.
tips.quote: I think to be a good inkastare you must learn to throw pretty even all the time, and how to clean up the mess when a kubb doesn't land where you planned.
tips.attributionLong: Johan Lundin, Signalgatan (2010 World Championship runners-up)

tips.body: Aim for a clean 180-degree end-over-end rotation on every baton. Consistency in rotation matters far more than how hard you throw.
tips.attributionLong: Kubb Tips, Strategies, and Rules — tyrstrekubb.com

tips.body: Lock your elbow on the throw. A steady arm makes it easier to aim and dramatically improves accuracy.
tips.attributionLong: Kubb Tips, Strategies, and Rules — tyrstrekubb.com

tips.body: Stand perpendicular to the kubb you're targeting — flat side facing you. That's the widest target and your best chance of impact.
tips.sourceTitle: 5 Tips to Always Win with Kubb

tips.body: It's more important to use less power and be accurate than to throw hard and miss completely. Every miss costs you more than a soft hit.
When things go badly, do not change your technique. Keep throwing the same way — inconsistency punishes you more than a bad streak.
tips.attributionLong: Kubb Tips, Strategies, and Rules — tyrstrekubb.com

tips.body: Aim at the nearest field kubbs first. If any survive, you want your opponent's advantage line to be as far back as possible.
tips.attributionLong: How to Play Kubb: A Beginner's Guide — freshhobby.com
tips.sourceTitle: How to Play Kubb: A Beginner's Guide

tips.body: Watch good throwers' grip, release point, and follow-through. Each new opponent teaches you a different way to handle pressure.
Grip the baton firmly but not tight. Too tight kills accuracy, too loose loses control. Hold near one end and keep your arm relaxed.
If you need one perfect shot to win, let your most accurate teammate take it. Save your best thrower for critical moments.
tips.attributionLong: Kubb Game and Rules Guide — funattic.com

tips.body: Every match comes down to baselines. Maximize the batons you get to throw at 8m by clearing field kubbs first — that's how you stay alive.
tips.quote: You need as many batons at eight meters as possible. To stay alive in any match, the bottom line is eliminating baseline kubbs.

tips.body: Hit the top front corner of the kubb with the baton angled between vertical and horizontal — that's the kubb's weakest spot and creates chaos.
Read the pile before you throw. Tight piles want power. Loose piles want finesse. Don't bring power to a finesse problem.
Every successful blast comes down to three things: power, rotation, and alignment. Miss any one of them and the baton goes nowhere useful.
Throw with light to medium power. It is much more important to hit one kubb than to try and take out two or three and miss completely.
The most important key to blasting is not missing — at all costs. The fastest way to miss is to throw too hard.
A complete miss makes you anxious — and that anxiety pushes the next baton too hard. Stay calm. Stick to your technique. Don't snowball.
The lower to the ground you hit the kubb, the harder you need to throw to make it go anywhere. Aim higher and let the spin work for you.
When you get an advantage line, use it. Knock down as many baseline kubbs as possible before your opponent can recover.
tips.attributionLong: Mastering Kubb: The Ultimate Guide — elakaioutdoor.com

tips.body: Practice taking out three- and four-kubb piles, not just big eight-kubb piles. The 3–4 cluster is what you'll actually see in real matches.
The next level of blasting is consistently knocking down shorts thrown far back. It minimizes the cost of a miss and sets up baseline doubles.
tips.quote: I am convinced that, in the future, the best teams need to master throwing the 'shorts' as far away as possible with a consistent hit rate knocking them down.
tips.attributionLong: Terry Ekelöf, Team Ekeby (9-time World Champion)

tips.body: Backyard kubb is purely skill. Tournament kubb is 60% physical and 40% mental — the mental game is what separates the contenders.
tips.quote: In tournaments (large spectator) games it is somewhere 60% physical (if you by physical mean pure playing the game ability) and 40% mental.
tips.attributionLong: Terry Ekelöf, Team Ekeby (9-time World Champion)

tips.body: Elite players remember losses better than wins. That's why we play not to lose — focus on minimizing mistakes, not maximizing performance.
tips.quote: I usually don't remember the wins very well, the losses stick so much better which makes us (at least me and my brother to play mainly NOT to lose).
tips.attributionLong: Terry Ekelöf, Team Ekeby (9-time World Champion)

tips.body: Whether you're winning or losing, act like everything's going according to plan. The calmer you seem, the more pressure your opponent feels.
At the elite level, matches are decided by which inkastare blinks first. The team that makes the fewest mistakes under pressure wins.
At elite level, even a single drilling mistake can cost you the match. Every throw counts. Treat practice with the same intensity.
Kubb is special because it's so hard to make perfect. You will never master it. That's the point — keep showing up.
tips.quote: What is so special about kubb? Hmm, I dont know...maybe that it is so hard to make perfect.
tips.attributionLong: Terry Ekelöf, Team Ekeby (9-time World Champion)

tips.body: For me, accuracy starts with removing all other thoughts from my head. Each throw is a small meditation — empty mind, clean release.
tips.quote: For me accuracy starts with removing all other thoughts from my head.
tips.attributionLong: Kubbnation Magazine, section on the mental game

tips.body: Friendly trash talk can shake your opponent's strategy — but only if you stay calm yourself. Confidence is the real weapon, not the words.
Practice 8m by throwing at the corner pins instead of kubbs. They're much narrower — hitting two of six is already good accuracy.
Set six corner pins at varied distances from 4m to 8m. Throw six batons, one per pin. Trains the often-neglected 5–7m range.
Inkast three or four kubbs into a pile, then try to take them out with one or two batons. This 3–4 cluster is the most common real-match scenario.
Push past your level by making practice harder than real matches. Build games that mimic kubb situations under pressure.
Come spring, work on drilling almost exclusively for a few weeks before picking up the batons. The inkast is the part that rusts first.
tips.quote: Come spring when the snow finally thaws here in Minneapolis, I work on drilling almost exclusively for a few weeks before picking up the batons.
tips.attributionLong: Eric Goplin, Tad Kubbler (3-time top-5 U.S. finisher)

tips.body: Stack three kubbs at 4m like London Bridge — two vertical with one horizontal on top. Throw soft and low to slide a baton through.
tips.attributionLong: Skill Builder Wednesday: Week One — kubbon.com

tips.body: Every field kubb left standing becomes your opponent's advantage line. Clearing them all is the single most important priority of the turn.
tips.attributionLong: How to Play Kubb: A Beginner's Guide — freshhobby.com
tips.sourceTitle: How to Play Kubb: A Beginner's Guide

tips.body: Every player should practice every role. Even the best inkastare or blaster has an off day — versatility wins more matches than specialization.
tips.attributionLong: New to Kubb: Team Roles — kubbon.com

tips.body: Elite inkasting takes Gladwell's 10,000 hours. It's a craft — no shortcuts. Show up, throw, repeat.
tips.quote: To be an elite inkastare, it takes Gladwell's 10,000 hours of practice. It's craftsmanship, and as such, it only comes with practice and experience from competing at high(est) level. No short cuts, sorry!
tips.attributionLong: Sören Wallin, Stockholm/Gotland player and kubb researcher

tips.body: Kubb isn't only a competition — it's a folkfest. Everyone can play. Don't lose that spirit even when you're playing your hardest.
tips.quote: Everyone can play it, old, young, handicapped, boy, girl, and so on. And its not only a competition. It's a festival or as I say in Swedish 'folkfest'.
